coredata.dc:title: Construction of carbon quantum dots/proton-functionalized graphitic carbon nitride nanocomposite via electrostatic self-assembly strategy and its application
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2016 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Jian, Xuan
Liu, Xian
Yang, Hui-min
Li, Jia-gang
Song, Xiu-li
Dai, Hong-yan
Liang, Zhen-hai
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), as advanced metal-free material catalysts have been the focus of considerable attention because of their superior photocatalytic activities. In this study, we developed a novel approach to obtain CQDs/g-C3N4 nanocomposite with effective interfacial contact by incorporating negatively charged CQDs and tailor-made proton-functionalized g-C3N4 via the electrostatic self-assembly strategy. Then, the morphology and microstructure of the new nanocomposite were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The CQDs and proton-functionalized g-C3N4 nanocomposite exhibited excellent electron transfer properties though electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), significantly enhanced photoactivity in the photoelectrochemical i–t curve test and degradation of methylene blue solution under visible light irradiation. These results demonstrated that the electrostatic self-assembly strategy process is a promising method of fabricating uniform metal-free material catalysts for an extensive range of applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Proton-functionalized g-C3N4
Electrostatic self-assembly strategy
Metal-free material catalysts
Photoactivity

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is jointly funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China and Shenhua Group Corp (Grant nos. U1261103 ), and the Open Fund of State Key Laboratory of Coal Conversion, Institute of Coal Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Science (No. J12-13-913 ). The authors thank anonymous reviewers for their helpful suggestions on the quality improvement of our present paper.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustrate of formation process of CQDs/HpCN via a pyrolysis, reflux and electrostatic self-assembly strategy. (For interpretation of the references to color in the text, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The pure CQDs solution sample (left) and CQDs absorbed on HpCN in DI water (right).
Zeta potential of g-C3N4, HpCN and CQDs/HpCN dispersed in DI water.

TEM images of (A) g-C3N4, (B) HpCN, (C) CQDs/HpCN and the insert of (D) shows the enlarged of TEM image corresponding to the yellow rectangle from the image shown in (C). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
XRD of patterns of g-C3N4, HpCN, CQDs/HpCN.
(A) FT-IR spectra of different samples: (a) g-C3N4, (b) HpCN and (c) CQDs/HpCN. (B) Enlarged FT-IR spectra corresponding to the gray rectangle from the image shown in panel (A).
(A) XPS survey spectra and high resolution XPS spectra of (B) C 1s, (C) O 1s and (D) N 1s for the (a) HpCN and (b) CQDs/HpCN.
UV–vis DRS of (a) CQDs/HpCN, (b) HpCN and (c) g-C3N4. (For interpretation of the references to color in the text, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
EIS Nyquist plots of different samples modified electrodes: (a) g-C3N4, (b) HpCN and (c) CQDs/HpCN.
Schematic representation of charge transport in the CQDs/HpCN nanocomposite and transient photocurrent response for pure g-C3N4, HpCN and CQDs/HpCN.
The photocatalytic degradation of MB dye (A) and recycling in the repeated MB degradation experiments with HpCN/CQDs under visible light irradiation (B).

head.ce:title: Construction of carbon quantum dots/proton-functionalized graphitic carbon nitride nanocomposite via electrostatic self-assembly strategy and its application

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, Shanxi, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), as advanced metal-free material catalysts have been the focus of considerable attention because of their superior photocatalytic activities. In this study, we developed a novel approach to obtain CQDs/g-C3N4 nanocomposite with effective interfacial contact by incorporating negatively charged CQDs and tailor-made proton-functionalized g-C3N4 via the electrostatic self-assembly strategy. Then, the morphology and microstructure of the new nanocomposite were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The CQDs and proton-functionalized g-C3N4 nanocomposite exhibited excellent electron transfer properties though electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), significantly enhanced photoactivity in the photoelectrochemical i–t curve test and degradation of methylene blue solution under visible light irradiation. These results demonstrated that the electrostatic self-assembly strategy process is a promising method of fabricating uniform metal-free material catalysts for an extensive range of applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past years, various metal materials, such as TiO2 [1], Cu2O [2] and the hottest perovskite [3] have been developed as photoelectrocatalyst for converting sunlight into electricity. However, poor durability, high cost and scarce resources hinder the widespread application of metal catalysts. To overcome these problems, metal-free material catalysts as a new class of catalytic materials have been the focus of considerable attention owing to their advantages, such as low cost, abundant resources, simple preparation and suitability for industrial-scale production [4]. Among the metal-free catalyst, two-dimensional (2D) graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) synthesized via an efficiency method of pyrolysis urea, dicyandiamide and melamine has been extensively applied in the catalysis field. g-C3N4 is regarded as one of the nitrogen-doped carbon materials, that have shown unique electronic, optical and photoelectric properties. In 2009, Wang et al. first reported that g-C3N4 has excellent photocatalytic ability [5]. Since then g-C3N4 has been known as a new generation of metal-free semiconductor for catalytic reactions, such as photoelectric conversion [6], and photocatalytic H2 evolution from water [7,8].

However, pure g-C3N4 suffers from several disadvantages, such as rapid recombination of a small specific area, photo-generated electron–hole pairs, slight solubility, and low visible light utilization efficiency [9]. To solve these problems, various methods have been developed. On one hand, doping g-C3N4 with carbon-based nanomaterials, such as grapheme [10,11], carbon nanotube [12,13] and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [14,15], can improve the photo-electrochemical performance. On the other hand, the acid-protonated g-C3N4 can successfully dissolve in water owing to the induced electrostatic repulsion and the intercalation of separate interlayers [16], such that the g-C3N4 colloids exhibit high charge transport stability and high photo-electrochemical properties [8]. These results demonstrate that doping and modification can be efficient approach to increase the photo-electrochemical properties of pure g-C3N4.

CQDs as a new member of the carbon-based nanomaterial family have recently been the focus of considerable scientific attention due to their unique and excellent physical and chemical properties. Graphene is a one-atom-thick 2D carbon material, that excellent electronic conductivity. Compared with a small size graphene, the quantum confinement and edge effects of zero-dimensional (0D) CQDs is the most important feature [17,18]. Owing to their high aqueous solubility, strong photoluminescence emission, desirable fluorescence properties and low cytotoxicity, CQDs have been extensively applied in various fields of studies, such as photocatalysis [19], biosensing [20] and chemiluminescence analysis [21].

In this paper, the 0D/2D CQDs/HpCN nanocomposite has many intriguing photoactivity properties. The photo-electrochemical i–t curves show that this nanocomposite exhibits a superior photoelectric conversion efficiency compared with pure g-C3N4 and HpCN, respectively. We also investigated the photocatalytic activity of different samples by evaluating the degradation of methylene blue (MB) solution. The possible mechanisms of the enhanced photocatalytic performance are the exceptional 0D/2D morphological structure associated with CQDs and HpCN and intimate interfacial coupling, which successfully harness the electron conductivity of CQDs to increase the efficiency of electron–cavity separation. In general, this study not only highlights the utilization of CQDs/HpCN as an ideal metal-free catalyst for various applications, but also emphasizes the rational significance of the well-contacted 0D/2D heterojunction interface between CQDs and HpCN by employing a simple electrostatic self-assembly approach on the basis of the electrostatic interaction.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was conducted with the Tecnai G220 (FEI, USA) operating at 200kV. The crystalline phases was examined with an X-ray diffraction (XRD) instrument (D/Max-2500, Rigaku, Japan) using Cu-Kα radiation (λ =0.15406nm) within the 2θ range from 10° to 90°. The light absorption properties of the samples were recorded on an ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectrophotometer (UV-2450, Shimadzu Corporation, Japan). The UV–vis absorption spectra (in the diffuse reflectance spectra mode) were recorded in the solid state, and BaSO4 powder was used as the substrate. Elemental compositions of the samples were detected by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi, Thermo Scientific, USA). Zeta potential measurements of these samples were obtained by dynamic light-scattering analysis using the Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern Instruments, England). In detail, the suspensions of each sample in aqueous solution media (0.1mgmL−1) were prepared and measured separately at room temperature. The molecular structural information was determined by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR; Model IR-Prestige-21, Shimadzu Corporation, Japan) using a standard KBr pellet method. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis used the VMP2 potentiostat controlled with EC-Lab software (Princeton, USA).

Photocurrent measurements were performed on an electrochemical analyzer (CHI660D, CHI Instruments, China) in a standard three-electrode configuration with Pt wire as the counter electrode and Hg/Hg2Cl2 (in saturated KCl) as the reference electrode. The working electrodes were fabricated as follows: the 10mg samples was mixed with 1mL deionised (DI) water to make a slurry, the slurry was dropped onto an ITO glass electrode with a 1cm×1cm area and the electrolytes were dried in a vacuum oven at 60°C for 8h. Irradiation proceeded with a Xe arc lamp. Na2SO4 (1.0M) aqueous solution was used as the electrolyte.

All chemical reagents were of analytical grade and the water used in the experiment was DI water (>18.5MΩcm resistivity). Pure g-C3N4 was synthesized by pyrolysis urea method based on the previous reports [24,25]. Briefly, 15.0g urea powder was placed in a semi-closed ceramic crucible with a cover and calcined at 520°C for 2.5h with the furnace heating rate of 10°Cmin−1. After the crucible was cooled to room temperature, the light yellow bulk g-C3N4 was collected and ground into powder form.

The preparation of HpCN was similarly to that previously reported in the literature [12]. Typically, 500mg g-C3N4 was added to 50mL concentrated HNO3 solution, stirred and refluxed at 80°C for 3h. Then, the mixture was diluted with 500mL DI water and washed several times. Afterward, the mixture was dried in a vacuum oven at 60°C for 12h, collected and ground into powder form.

CQDs were synthesized by the typical direct electrochemical method, as reported in the literature [26,27]. In detail, two graphite rods set as anode and cathode were inserted into DI water. Then, a 30V potential was applied between the two electrodes. After continuous stirring for 120h, a dark yellow solution was obtained. The resultant solution was centrifuged to remove the precipitated graphite oxide and graphite particles. The obtained solution was water soluble CQDs.

The CQDs/HpCN was fabricated by the electrostatic adsorption technique using negatively charged CQDs and positively charged HpCN in combination. Firstly, 100mg as-prepared HpCN powder was added to the previously obtained 100mL CQDs solution ultrasound-treated for 15min. Then, the mixture was left to stand for approximately 48h. Afterward, the resultant solution was centrifuged at 10,000rpm for 10min and washed with DI water several times to remove the unabsorbed CQDs. Finally, the mixture was dried in a vacuum oven at 60°C for 12h, collected and ground into powder form.

The CQDs/HpCN nanocomposite was fabricated by a pyrolysis urea at 520°C for 2.5h, refluxed in concentrated HNO3 at 80°C for 3h and underwent the three steps of the electrostatic self-assembly strategy as illustrated in Scheme 1 . A previous study reported that a considerable amount of COO− and OH− can be observed on the surface of CQDs [23], which indicated that CQDs possessed negative polarity on its surface in the solution. To absorb negative polarity CQDs, g-C3N4 was protonated by concentrated HNO3 to obtain HpCN. It indicated that HpCN has positive polarity and dispersed better than the bulk g-C3N4 in the solution. It is clear that CQDs and HpCN can be combined together easily via electrostatic attraction self-assembly strategy (in Fig. 1: the left side shows the pure CQDs solution and the right side shows the CQDs/HpCN hybrid in water solution). Thus, color modification of pure g-C3N4 (yellow), HpCN (white) and CQDs/HpCN (black) (Scheme 1) powders further indicates that CQDs were successfully absorbed on the surface of HpCN.

To investigate the formation process of the composites, the zeta potential of the materials was tested. The results are showed in Fig. 2 zeta potential of −19.8mV indicates that g-C3N4 possessed negative polarity on its surface in the solution. For the purpose of absorbing the negative polarity CQDs, g-C3N4 (Scheme 1, yellow powder) should be modified into positive polarity. Then, the two materials can be combined together effectively in the solution through electrostatic attraction. We combined the protonated g-C3N4 with concentrated HNO3 to obtain the HpCN sample (Scheme 1, white powder) to solve the problem. The zeta potential of HpCN is +52.6mV, as shown in Fig. 2, which indicated that CQDs and HpCN can be combined together through the electrostatic self-assembly strategy (Scheme 1, black powder). The zeta potential decreased from +52.6mV (HpCN) to +24.2mV (CQDs/HpCN) because the amount of CQDs added was small and did not cover the entire surface of HpCN, such that part of H+ still existed on the surface of HpCN, which kept the nanocomposite in positive polarity as a whole. The previously presented analysis further validated that CQDs were successfully absorbed on the surface of HpCN through the electrostatic self-assembly strategy.

The microscopic morphologies of g-C3N4, HpCN and CQDs/HpCN were characterized by TEM. As depicted in Fig. 3 A, an abundant heterogeneous porous structure in terms of size and shape was highly developed and extensively distributed on the surface of pure g-C3N4 during the thermal polymerization of urea [11]. The diameter of the pure g-C3N4 porous structure is several tens of nanometers. With the concentrated HNO3 solution pretreatment of g-C3N4, the morphology of HpCN was exhibited a thin-layered structure that is completely different from pure g-C3N4 (Fig. 3B), which indicated that HpCN was dispersed better than pure g-C3N4 in DI water solution in previous report [28–30]. The TEM images of CQDs/HpCN composites characterization are shown in Fig. 3C and D. Notably, CQDs (the diameter of CQDs was approximately 5nm, as shown in the inset image of Fig. 3D) were embedded in a thin film of HpCN (denoted by the yellow dashed line), which was significantly different from pure HpCN (Fig. 3B). The TEM images can fully prove that HpCN was effectively and completely coupled with negatively charged CQDs via the electrostatic self-assembly strategy process.

The crystal structures of g-C3N4, HpCN and CQDs/HpCN were determined by XRD, and the results are shown in Fig. 4 . Two characteristic XRD peaks of the g-C3N4 crystal phase are revealed, one peak (CN 100) at about 13.18° and the other peak at approximately 27.48°. Fig. 4 shows the two typical diffraction peaks of g-C3N4, the peak (CN 002) originating from the interlayer reflection of a graphite-like crystal structure and the peak (CN 100) originating from the in-plane structural repeating motifs of the aromatic systems. In Fig. 4, only one sharp diffraction peak (CN 002) of HpCN and CQDs/HpCN is observed, which reveal that the in-plane periodicity of the aromatic systems have been destroyed through the breakage of the hydrogen bonds by concentrated HNO3 protonation [12]. It is clear that main diffraction peak (CN 002) of HpCN and CQDs/HpCN shifted to a higher degree, which completely proved that HpCN and the hybrid loose the layered structure swollen by H+ insertion from HNO3 [12]. The slightly low diffraction peak of CQDs/HpCN and HpCN can be attributed to the trace amount of the loaded CQDs.

The interaction between the g-C3N4, HpCN and CQDs/HpCN was investigated by FT-IR spectra. It is clear that the g-C3N4 (Fig. 5 a) spectra contains several major bands between 600cm−1 and 1800cm−1, which is in good agreement with the overall g-C3N4 features in the previous literatures [12,13,31,32]. As shown in Fig. 5B, all spectra displayed the sharp absorption band at approximately 812cm−1 could be attributed to the breathing mode of tri-s-triazine units [34]. Several strong absorption bands in the range of 1200–1600cm−1 region are corresponding to the stretching vibration of the CN heterocycle with peaks positioned at 1248, 1323, 1456, 1570 and 1635cm−1, comprising both N(C)3 and CNHC units. This evidenced the formation of extended network of CNC bonds [24]. The broad peak at about 3180cm−1 is assigned to the NH and OH stretches due to the free amino group and adsorbed hydroxyl species. For sample g-C3N4, the intensities of bond at 1248, 1323 and 1570cm−1 seem to be strong as compared to those in HpCN and CQDs/HpCN, suggesting the CN of g-C3N4 has changed after it was protonated by concentrated HNO3. Notably, the bonds at 812, 1456 and 1635cm−1 in g-C3N4 illustrated a slight red-shift to a lower wavenumber of ca. 807, 1449, 1616cm−1 in the CQDs/HpCN composite, indicating the bond strength of CN is weakened due to the strong interaction between the CQDs and HpCN [35] formed by an electrostatic self-assembly approach process, which is consistent with above zeta potential, TEM and XRD results. The interaction between the HpCN and CQDs may benefit the electron transfer and hence enhance the photocatalytic activity of the composite material.

To comprehend the interactions and chemical status of the constituent elements in the g-C3N4, HpCN and CQDs/HpCN samples, the XPS spectra are further performed (Fig. 6 ). In Fig. 6A, the XPS survey spectra depicted that the C, N, O elements were detected on the g-C3N4, HpCN and CQDs/HpCN samples. For detail information, the high XPS resolution of C 1s (Fig. 6B) can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 288.3eV, 285.8eV and 284.8eV. The major C peak at 288.3eV is identified as sp2-bonded carbon (NCN), and the weaker one at 284.8eV corresponds to graphitic carbon, which is usually observed in the XPS spectrum of carbon nitrides. In addition, the peak at 284.8eV for CQDs/HpCN (Fig. 6B-b) is bigger than the peak for HpCN (Fig. 6B-a), that may be assigned to the adventitious carbon or sp2 bonded CC bonds of graphitic carbon from the CQDs [15,33,34]. This result coincided well with the high resolution O 1s spectrum of the CQDs/HpCN (Fig. 6C-b), in which the deconvoluted peaks centered at 531.6 and 532.4eV corresponded to HOCO and COH, respectively [38]. The only one peak at 532.4eV in the HpCN sample (Fig. 6C-a) was ascribed to the adsorbed O species on the surface of the HpCN, presumably due to the protonation process by concentration HNO3 which is a common phenomenon found in literature [39,40].

As shown in Fig. 6D-a, the high resolution N 1s spectra displayed four deconvolution peaks with binding energies of 398.8, 399.9, 400.8 and 406.6eV, demonstrating four types of N bonding in the samples of HpCN. N 1s peak at 398.8eV are characteristics that corresponds to sp2 hybridized aromatic N bonded to carbon atoms in the triazine units (CNC), and the peak at 399.9eV is assigned to the tertiary N bonded to carbon atoms in the form of N(C)3 or HN(C)2 as the previous report [15,39]. The weaker peak with a high binding energy at 401.2eV is attributed to quaternary N bonded to three carbon atoms in the aromatic cycles (CNH) due to incomplete condensation during the thermal polymerization process [40]. The peak centered at 406.6eV corresponds to the charging effects or positive charge localization in heterocycles due to π-excitations and protonation [41,42]. On the other hand, the dominant peaks in CQDs/HpCN sample (Fig. 6D-b) illustrated slight shift to higher binding energies of ca. 399, 400.1 and 400.9eV, indicating an electronic interaction between CQDs and HpCN is formed between these two components, developing a hybrid heterojunction in the CQDs/HpCN, which is consistent with our FT-IR results [11,24]. The weak N 1s peak at 406.6eV (Fig. 6D-b) shows a substantial decrease from HpCN to CQDs/HpCN can be attributed to the decreasing of positive charge localization in heterocycles, which correlated well with our zeta potential results. On the basis of above TEM, XRD, FT-IR and XPS analyses, a strong coupling and hybridization between CQDs and HpCN is evidenced.

To investigate the synergetic effect of CQDs and HpCN on the light absorption properties, the DRS measurement of the different samples (CQDs/HpCN, HpCN and g-C3N4) are shown in Fig. 7 . It is clearly to see that the g-C3N4 absorption edge is 450nm, the blue-shift absorption edge is 415nm and the decreasing absorption intensity of HpCN are consistent with color change of yellow to white. By contrast, the CQDs/HpCN composite exhibits a strong absorption peak that extends from the broad background absorption region the visible light region, which indicates that CQDs/HpCN can absorb more energy from visible-light that leads to enhanced photoactivity and is in agreement with the color change of the corresponding samples from light yellow to black (Fig. 7a–c). A further observation is that CQDs/HpCN exhibits almost the same absorption edge as HpCN, suggesting CQDs are not incorporated to the lattice of HpCN, and is only immobilized on the surface of HpCN via electrostatic self-assembly approach.

To understand the electrochemical performance of different samples modified electrodes, EIS measurement was conducted to investigate the charge transfer processes on the electrode and at the contact interface between electrode and electrolyte, and to assess the separation efficiency of charge carriers [43]. The EIS Nyquist plots in Fig. 8 b and c show the comparison between HpCN and CQDs/HpCN modified electrodes and the pure g-C3N4 electrode (Fig. 8a). A significant decrease in the semicircular Nyquist plots is observed on the g-C3N4 electrode. To the best of our knowledge, in the EIS Nyquist plot, the diameter of the semicircle has been considered as the charge transfer representing the rate of electron transport and transfer between the electrolyte solution and the composite at their electrochemical interface at the high frequencies [43–45]. This finding can be attributed to the poor electrical conductivity of the semiconductor g-C3N4. Evidently, when CQDs/HpCN was modified on the electrode surface, its Ret value was smaller than that of other modified electrodes. This result demonstrated that the CQDs/HpCN nanocomposite has been successfully assembled via electrostatic self-assembly approach, which exhibited efficient conductivity for the electron transfer process.

The excellent photocatalytic performance of CQDs/HpCN can be further enhanced because of the lower rate of recombination and more efficient separation of photogenerated electron–hole pairs for the interface of CQDs and HpCN. Thus, the results provided more evidence to illustrate that g-C3N4 protonated by concentrated HNO3 and CQDs played important roles in improving the properties of this photocatalytic reaction. Therefore, the electrostatic self-assembly strategy process of HpCN with CQDs is a promising method of synthesizing the photoelectrochemical activity of a hybrid material with improved photoelectrochemical properties.

In summary, we have demonstrated a simple and efficiency method to obtain the novel metal-free catalyst CQDs/HpCN by electrostatic self-assembly approach via a facile surface charge modification and protonation of g-C3N4 with concentrated HNO3. By taking advantage of the excellent conductivity of CQDs/HpCN, it is used as photoelectrode to enhance the photoelectrochemical properties, which are due to the synergetic interactions between CQDs and HpCN. The present study provides a novel method to prepare CQDs/HpCN and extend several new approaches for application to specific tasks, such as in the oxygen reduction reaction, hydrogen evolution and CO2 capture reduction reaction catalyst. Finally, we hope that this environmental and convenient synthesis process could be extended to the development of other metal-free catalyst processes in the future.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is jointly funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China and Shenhua Group Corp (Grant nos. U1261103), and the Open Fund of State Key Laboratory of Coal Conversion, Institute of Coal Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Science (No. J12-13-913). The authors thank anonymous reviewers for their helpful suggestions on the quality improvement of our present paper.
